elements.sent-2411-001: To address this issue, we introduce a definition of precision that weights $\mathrm{FP _{\mathrm{unknown }$ by the proportion of objects in the subset: \begin{equation}\mathrm{Prec}_{\mathrm{subset}} = \frac{\mathrm{TP}_{\mathrm{subset}}}{\mathrm{TP}_{\mathrm{subset}} + \mathrm{FP}_{\mathrm{subset}} + \frac{N_{\mathrm{subset}}}{N_{\mathrm{total}}}\cdot\mathrm{FP}_{\mathrm{unknown}}}\end{equation} $N_{\mathrm{subset }$ and $N_{\mathrm{total }$ are the number of objects in the subset and the total number of objects, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: The definition of recall remains unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-003: In contrast to the standard Waymo metric, this formulation allows the comparison and combination of subset APs, as illustrated in~\Cref{tab:subset_ap .}
elements.sent-2411-004: We note, in particular, that when using our formulation the weighted average of subset APs weighted by subset size is closer to the AP of the union of the subsets.
elements.sent-2411-005: CenterPoint~\cite{yin2021center and VoxelNeXt~\cite{chen2023voxelnext are both single stage CNN-based 3D object detectors.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The input point cloud first undergoes voxelization and is then fed to a sparse convolutional backbone.
elements.sent-2411-007: Multiple detection heads are used to separately produce bounding box attributes, including confidence score, location, and box dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-008: DSVT~\cite{wang2023dsvt is an emerging transformer-based 3D object detector.}
elements.sent-2411-009: In DSVT, the traditional convolution backbone is replaced with multiple transformer blocks consisting of shifted window and partition-based self-attention operations.
elements.sent-2411-010: Following~\cite{caesar2020nuscenes , we add a two-layer regression head to predict an object's velocity vector for all architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-011: For DSVT, we use the pillar variant (which we denote DSVT-P).
elements.sent-2411-012: As the dynamic voxelization adopted by the original work cannot be scaled beyond 4-frame aggregation on our hardware, we use a traditional static voxelization where for each voxel, at most 40 points are randomly selected and processed.
elements.sent-2411-013: To make the computation tractable, we further reduce the input channels from 192 to 96, and the hidden channels from 384 to 192.
elements.figure-2411-001: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.4em} \caption{Overall vehicle AP on the Waymo validation split. The best performance is in bold and the second best is underlined.} \label{tab:results-overall} 0.75\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|llllllllllllll} VADet \multicolumn{1}{c}{3f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{4f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{5f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{6f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{7f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{8f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{9f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{10f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{11f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{12f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{13f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{14f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{15f} \multicolumn{1}{c}{16f} CenterPoint 71.5 68.1 68.9 69.5 70.0 70.3 70.5 70.7 70.8 70.9 {\ul 71.0} {\ul 71.0} {\ul 71.0} {\ul 71.0} 70.9 VoxelNeXt 76.5 73.4 74.1 74.6 75.0 75.3 75.4 75.6 75.7 75.7 {\ul 75.8} {\ul 75.8} {\ul 75.8} {\ul 75.8} 75.7 DSVT-P 74.5 69.5 70.3 70.8 71.4 71.7 72.0 72.2 72.4 72.6 72.7 72.8 73.0 73.0 {\ul 73.2} \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-014: The baseline models are trained on the entire Waymo training split for 20 epochs across 8 NVIDIA A6000 GPUs using the proposed RAT strategy.
elements.sent-2411-015: We use the Adam optimizer and a one-cycle learning rate schedule, with an initial learning rate of 0.0003 and a maximum learning rate of 0.003.
elements.sent-2411-016: We use a total batch size of 32 for CenterPoint, 32 for VoxelNeXt, and 16 for DSVT-P.
elements.sent-2411-017: We initialize our VADet models with the weights from the baseline models, then fine-tune them for an additional epoch using cosine learning rate decay with an initial learning rate of 0.0001.
elements.sent-2411-018: Since our models rely on information cached from previous frames, including point clouds and predictions, the standard frame-based shuffling cannot be applied during training.
elements.sent-2411-019: Instead, we divide the dataset into mini-sequences with a maximum of 32 frames and shuffle the mini-sequences.
elements.sent-2411-020: This introduces randomness while ensuring that frames appear in their correct sequence.
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, as the performance of a model can fluctuate during training (due to ongoing optimization), following~\cite{fan2023fsd++ , we load the offline predictions from the baseline models using 3-frame fixed aggregation during training.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We implement $\eta$ as a lookup table: we subdivide the training dataset based on speed and point cloud density and empirically determine the frame count that leads to the highest average precision for each subcategory (\cref{sec:va ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: They are chosen based on the training set object statistics to ensure a sufficient number of objects in each bin for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-025: For each speed and density combination, we evaluate the baseline model on 3--16-frame input and select the frame count with the highest AP performance.
elements.sent-2411-026: Background points undergo a fixed 3-frame aggregation.
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Overall vehicle performance on the Waymo validation split compared with SOTA multi-frame detectors.} 0.9\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c} Method \# frames L1 AP/APH L2 AP/APH PillarNeXt-B~\cite{li2023pillarnext} 3 80.6/80.1 72.9/72.4 DSVT-pillar~\cite{wang2023dsvt} 4 81.7/81.2 73.8/73.4 DSVT-voxel~\cite{wang2023dsvt} 4 81.8/81.4 74.1/73.6 FSD++~\cite{fan2023fsd++} 7 81.4/80.9 73.3/72.9 CenterFormer~\cite{zhou2022centerformer} 8 78.8/78.3 74.3/73.8 3D-MAN~\cite{yang20213dman} 16 74.5/74.0 67.6/67.1 MPPNet~\cite{chen2022mppnet} 16 {\ul 82.7/82.3} {\ul 75.4/75.0} VADet-CenterPoint 3--16 79.0/78.5 71.7/71.3 VADet-DSVT-P 3--16 82.0/81.6 74.5/74.1 VADet-VoxelNeXt 3--16 83.9/83.4 76.6/76.1 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:results-sota}
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Overall vehicle performance on the Waymo test split compared with other methods (without TTA or ensemble).} 0.9\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c} Method \# frames Modality L2 AP/APH AFDetV2~\cite{hu2022afdetv2} 2 L 74.3/73.9 PV-RCNN++~\cite{shi2023pvrcnn++} 2 L 76.3/75.9 SWFormer~\cite{sun2022swformer} 3 L 75.0/74.7 PillarNeXt-B~\cite{li2023pillarnext} 3 L 76.2/75.8 FSD++~\cite{fan2023fsd++} 7 L 77.1/76.7 3D-MAN~\cite{yang20213dman} 16 L 70.4/70.0 MPPNet~\cite{chen2022mppnet} 16 L 77.3/76.9 CenterFormer~\cite{zhou2022centerformer} 16 L {\ul 78.7/78.3} BEVFusion~\cite{liu2023bevfusion} 3 C+L 77.9/77.5 DeepFusion~\cite{li2022deepfusion} 5 C+L 76.1/75.7 HorizonLiDAR3D~\cite{ding20201st} 5 C+L 78.2/77.8 LoGoNet~\cite{li2023logonet} 5 C+L {\ul 79.7/79.3} VADet-VoxelNeXt 3--16 L 79.8/79.4 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:results-sota-test}
elements.sent-2411-027: We present the overall performance of VADet compared to baseline models and SOTA methods in~\Cref{sec:results-overall .}
elements.sent-2411-028: To demonstrate the better trade-offs achieved by VADet, in~\Cref{sec:results-breakdown we perform a detailed evaluation based on speed and point cloud density.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, VADet using CenterPoint achieves 71.5 AP compared to 71.0 AP using 12, 13, 14, or 15-frame aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-030: For VoxelNeXt, VADet achieves 76.5 AP compared to 75.8 AP using 12, 13, 14, or 15-frame aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-031: DSVT-P with VADet achieves 74.5 AP compared to 73.2 AP using 16-frame aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-032: When compared with SOTA object detection methods in~\Cref{tab:results-sota and~\Cref{tab:results-sota-test , VADet-equipped models also have competitive performance on both the validation and test splits.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Most notably, our single-stage VADet-VoxelNeXt achieves 76.1 and 79.4 level 2 APH on the validation and test split respectively, outperforming two-stage multi-frame methods such as MPPNet~\cite{chen2022mppnet by a large margin, with lower computation overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, we measure that the second stage proposed by MPPNet introduces an additional 900--2500\,ms latency over the base detector, while VADet only requires an additional 50\,ms overhead for input aggregation, which we believe can be further optimized with a GPU-accelerated implementation.
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, with a powerful backbone such as VoxelNeXt, our method can match the performance of recent SOTA camera-LiDAR fusion methods such as LoGoNet~\cite{li2023logonet .}

elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/stationary_overall_vs_frames.pdf} \caption{Stationary} \label{fig:stationary_vs_frames} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/slow_overall_vs_frames.pdf} \caption{Slow} \label{fig:slow_vs_frames} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/fast_overall_vs_frames.pdf} \caption{Fast} \label{fig:fast_vs_frames} \end{subfigure} \caption{AP vs. the number of frames for stationary ($<$0.2\,m/s), slow ([0.2,10)\,m/s), and fast-moving ($\geq$10\,m/s) vehicles.} \label{fig:speed_vs_frames}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our results demonstrate that VADet can effectively utilize multiple frames to achieve SOTA detection performance, suggesting that by addressing various performance trade-offs with carefully constructed input, a simple single-stage object detection architecture such as VoxelNeXt can outperform much more complex SOTA methods.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/dynamic_sparse_vs_frames.pdf} \caption{Sparse} \label{fig:sparse_vs_frames} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/dynamic_medium_vs_frames.pdf} \caption{Medium} \label{fig:medium_vs_frames} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/dynamic_dense_vs_frames.pdf} \caption{Dense} \label{fig:dense_vs_frames} \end{subfigure} \caption{AP vs. the number of frames for sparse ($<$2\,pts/m$^2$), medium ((2,100]\,pts/m$^2$), and dense ($>$100 pts/m$^2$) dynamic vehicles.} \label{fig:density_vs_frames}
elements.sent-2411-037: Qualitative results comparing 3-frame and 16-frame fixed aggregation with VADet are shown in~\cref{fig:qualitative .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Overall, we observe that 16-frame input appears much denser than 3-frame and VADet.
elements.sent-2411-039: This is especially noticeable for background points, which may not be beneficial to detecting objects and introduce unnecessary computation.
elements.sent-2411-040: The 3-frame model is unable to detect the occluded stationary vehicles depicted in the upper images, due to the lack of points.
elements.sent-2411-041: While these objects appear more complete with 16 frames, the model can only partially detect them.
elements.sent-2411-042: We hypothesize this is due to the dense background points leading to confusion.
elements.sent-2411-043: VADet, on the other hand, aggregates only object points while keeping the background sparse, and therefore can accurately detect all vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-044: Similarly, for the fast-moving vehicles seen in the lower images, 16-frame aggregation results in long ``smudges'' around each vehicle, leading to inaccurate localization.
elements.sent-2411-045: In these situations, VADet does not over-aggregate these objects and benefits from the more accurate detections achieved by 3-frame input.
elements.sent-2411-046: The overall performance degradation at 16-frame fixed aggregation, if any, appears to be minuscule for the baseline models: just 0.1\% degradation for CenterPoint and VoxelNeXt.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is due to the extreme imbalance between different types of objects in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-048: For instance, the overall results are skewed in favour of the approximately 80\% stationary vehicles in the dataset, which negatively affects the performance of dynamic objects.
elements.sent-2411-049: As a result, we perform a breakdown analysis to highlight the performance trade-offs and demonstrate how VADet can benefit objects that are negatively impacted by fixed aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-050: To illustrate the impact of input aggregation on objects with different speeds, we divide the vehicle class into stationary ($<$0.2\,m/s), slow ([0.2,10)\,m/s), and fast ($\geq$10\,m/s) subcategories.
elements.sent-2411-051: Stationary, slow, and fast vehicles respectively amount to 79.7\%, 14.2\%, and 6.1\% of the Waymo validation set.
elements.sent-2411-052: The AP performance for each subcategory is reported in~\Cref{tab:results-speed .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Our results for the baseline models are consistent with the trade-off observed in existing work and further suggest that this effect is consistent across different architectures.
elements.sent-2411-054: Moreover, the results show that the optimal number of frames can be different for objects with different speeds.
elements.sent-2411-055: Specifically, the stationary vehicle performance (\cref{fig:stationary_vs_frames ) increases as more frames are used for aggregation: all baseline models achieve the best performance with 16-frame input, suggesting more aggregation is beneficial.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Slow vehicles (\cref{fig:slow_vs_frames ), on the other hand, reach maximum performance at 7 frames, while for fast-moving vehicles (\cref{fig:fast_vs_frames ), a large degradation can be observed when more frames are used, indicating aggregating fewer frames is more favourable.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: VADet, on the other hand, does not exhibit such a performance trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-058: For all three architectures and subcategories, VADet achieves higher AP than the best fixed aggregation configuration in each respective subcategory (underlined in~\Cref{tab:results-speed ).}
elements.sent-2411-059: This not only highlights the effectiveness of VADet at mitigating the performance trade-off between objects at different speeds but also demonstrates the applicability of VADet to various architectures.
elements.sent-2411-060: To demonstrate that input aggregation can also lead to a performance trade-off between objects with different point cloud densities, we evaluate and report the performance of vehicles with different point cloud densities.
elements.sent-2411-061: Using~\cref{eq:density , we divide the vehicle class into sparse ($<$2\,pts/m$^2$), medium ([2,100)\,pts/m$^2$), and dense ($\geq$100\,pts/m$^2$) subcategories.}
elements.sent-2411-062: For stationary objects, we observe the same trend seen in~\cref{fig:stationary_vs_frames , suggesting the performance is not influenced by the point cloud density.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The following analysis therefore focuses on dynamic objects ($\geq$0.2\,m/s), with the full results for stationary objects in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-064: The dynamic vehicles in the validation set contain 23.9\% sparse, 67.9\% medium, and 8.2\% dense vehicles according to our definition.
elements.sent-2411-065: The AP performance for each subcategory is detailed in~\Cref{tab:results-density .}
elements.sent-2411-066: While we have previously observed that dynamic objects favour fewer input frame counts, we notice in~\cref{fig:sparse_vs_frames that sparse objects can still benefit from more input aggregation: up to 7-frame aggregation for CenterPoint and VoxelNeXt, and up to 12-frame aggregation for DSVT-P.}
elements.sent-2411-067: On the other hand, for denser objects (\cref{fig:medium_vs_frames,fig:dense_vs_frames ), higher frame counts become harmful to the detection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-068: All three architectures achieve the best performance for medium density vehicles with 5-frame input, and dense vehicles with 3-frame input.
elements.sent-2411-069: Our results highlight a trade-off that has not been studied in existing work and underscores the necessity of considering point density in our proposed approach.
elements.sent-2411-070: Compared to fixed aggregation, VADet is comparable to the best performance in each subcategory, surpassing fixed aggregation in many cases.
elements.sent-2411-071: This demonstrates that VADet is also effective at addressing the trade-off between objects with different point densities for different architectures.
elements.sent-2411-072: Input aggregation adds information to sparse detections, up to the point that an object's motion or other characteristics cause confusion.
elements.sent-2411-073: VADet therefore improves the detection of certain objects by not over-aggregating them, but their detections may nevertheless be sparse and could benefit from aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-074: For such objects, the addition of a different aggregation approach would be necessary.
elements.sent-2411-075: In this work, we have identified speed and point density as important features to provide as inputs to function $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-076: We hypothesize that a future implementation of $\eta$ as a more abstract learned function from point clouds to numbers of frames will produce even better results.
elements.sent-2411-077: In the preceding text, we have focused on the Waymo vehicle class---a common expedient to simplify comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-078: In the supplementary material, we give further results that show VADet is also effective on the Waymo pedestrian class.
elements.sent-2411-079: While VADet's benefits will be class and dataset dependent, we do not anticipate any obvious limitations.
elements.sent-2411-080: We have addressed the inherent performance trade-off of fixed aggregation by proposing VADet, a variable aggregation approach that can be easily applied to different architectures with minimal modifications.
elements.sent-2411-081: Our extensive evaluation shows that VADet can effectively combat the trade-off and achieve SOTA performance.. \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}
elements.sent-2411-082: In response to the reviewers' comments, we have made a number of specific changes in the main text, with the responses to major comments summarized below.
elements.sent-2411-083: We have also clarified throughout the manuscript that VADet is not necessarily a competitor to alternative approaches, but rather an enhancement to existing architectures.
elements.sent-2411-084: No comparison with feature-level aggregation Response: We have included in Tables 4 and 5 comprehensive comparisons with SOTA methods using both naive aggregation and feature-level aggregation methods including 3D-MAN (CVPR\, '21) and MPPNet (ECCV\, '22), the latter with 16 frames being the current SOTA.
elements.sent-2411-085: We only include recent multi-frame and camera-lidar fusion solutions for test set results.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, we encourage the reviewer to check out the official leaderboard for comparisons with other older methods: https://waymo.com/open/challenges/detection-3d/results/544ed478-e609/1716000629014000/.
elements.sent-2411-087: The proposed method cannot handle cases where stationary and moving objects occur together.
elements.sent-2411-088: Response: Our method is in fact specifically designed to handle situations where dynamic and stationary objects coexist.
elements.sent-2411-089: In such situations, the stationary objects would undergo more aggregation compared to the dynamic objects.
elements.sent-2411-090: To clarify this, we have edited the introduction and included a qualitative example showcasing the resulting point clouds in Sec 5.2 and Fig. 2.
elements.sent-2411-091: The authors should provide more experiments to evaluate the computational cost of VADet.
elements.sent-2411-092: Response: We have incorporated the reviewer's suggestion and included latency measurements in Sec 5.1.
elements.sent-2411-093: Specifically, with our current implementation, we measured that the object-level aggregation on average introduces around 50\,ms of latency, which we believe can be further reduced with GPU acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-094: We argue our solution is significantly more efficient compared to feature-level methods such as MPPNet, which adds an additional 900-2500\,ms of computation on top of the base detector.
elements.sent-2411-095: We can achieve similar performance with much less overhead.
elements.sent-2411-097: Response: We fully acknowledge that evaluation on more datasets such as nuScenes would better demonstrate our proposed method's effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-098: However, we choose to instead allocate our limited resources to experiments with different architectures for two reasons: First, we would like to explore how different backbones react to input aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-099: While the effects of aggregation have been mentioned briefly and independently in other work, we present more comprehensive comparisons across different architectures and number of input frames.
elements.sent-2411-100: Second, and most importantly, we would like to contrast the architectural-specific methods such as MPPNet and emphasize that our method can be applied to object detectors with different backbones.
elements.sent-2411-102: Response: We have included qualitative examples in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-103: Experiments are only on the vehicle category.
elements.sent-2411-104: Response: We have incorporated the reviewer's comment into Sec.
elements.sent-2411-105: In the main text, we choose to place our focus on vehicles since pedestrian and cyclists have a considerably narrower speed range: they are rarely fully stationary, nor can they reach high speed compared to vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-106: Their deformable nature might require a separate in-depths analysis of other factors.
elements.sent-2411-107: This is unfortunately out-of-scope for the main text.
elements.sent-2411-108: The chosen of the hyper-parameters $\eta$ seems very ad-hoc and are specific to a certain dataset.
elements.sent-2411-109: Response: $\eta$ is not a hyper-parameter nor is it chosen arbitrarily or hand-crafted to provide us the best results.
elements.sent-2411-110: Instead, we follow the algorithm described in Sec 3.2.1 and automatically construct $\eta$ for each model/dataset during training.
elements.sent-2411-111: This process only uses the training data, and thus $\eta$ can be regarded as a function learned from the training set.
elements.sent-2411-112: To clarify this, we have edited the introduction to emphasize $\eta$'s role in our approach.
elements.sent-2411-115: It is better to provide comparisons with off-board detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-116: Response: Our method only uses up to 15 previous frames as input and is intended to be used in online settings where small overhead is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-117: It would therefore not be appropriate to compare with off-board detection methods that use full sequences and can derive information from future frames.
elements.sent-2411-118: However, we provide comparisons with other methods under similar settings in Table 4 and 5.
